TSAI ยท ERA V5 ยท Session 2 โ Tokenization

TokenSangam โ can 4 languages share 10,000 tokens?

Sangam (เคธเคเคเคฎ) = a confluence, where rivers meet โ and that is literally this tokenizer: 9,744 merges flowing English, Hindi, Telugu and Kannada into one byte-level BPE vocabulary, built from scratch on the India Wikipedia page. The score is 1000 รท (max โ min fertility), fertility = tokens per word. We built it twice: the first attempt hit a measured wall; the second changed one rule and broke through. Everything below is live โ drag, type, paste a Wikipedia page.

How the tokenizer was built

The BPE loop is four steps. The whole game is who picks the next merge โ and where merges are allowed to reach.

page text4 languages, cleaned
โ
256 bytesevery char = known bytes, zero UNK
โ
count neighbour pairswho sits together most?
โ
merge the winnertwo symbols โ one new token
โบ repeat 9,744 times โ vocab 10,000
Attempt 1 โ word-BPE (the wall)
Merges stop at word edges and go to whichever language is furthest from its gate. English needs 6,365 merges for 1.19; getting hi/te/kn under 1.8 needs 11,020 total โ only 9,744 exist. No split of the budget can fix arithmetic.
โ at 10k: en 1.19 ยท indic 2.06 โ score 1,150
Final โ phrase-BPE (the breakthrough)
One rule change: merges may cross spaces (split only at sentence ends โ the SentencePiece split_by_whitespace=false idea). One token can be " of the" or " เคเฅ เคฒเคฟเค" โ English's bill drops to 4,547, and the savings carry every language under its gate with margin. English freezes at 1.175 the moment it arrives โ it's the score's minimum (helping it would widen the gap), and parking 0.025 below the 1.2 gate absorbs live-page edits, since the grader checks against today's Wikipedia.
โ at 10k: en 1.17 ยท hi/te/kn 1.76 โ score 1,711 ยท every gate has drift headroom

Watch the same sentence get merged

vocab so far: 256

Attempt 1 ยท merges stop at spaces

Final ยท merges cross spaces

โฃ = space inside a token ยท ๏ฟฝ = a token that ends mid-character โ byte-level tokens don't have to respect character edges, the bytes still decode perfectly in sequence.

Drag the vocab budget โ everything recomputes

At slider V only the first Vโ256 merges exist. That's not a simulation: BPE learns merges in order, so the cut list is the tokenizer you'd get training to V (prefix property โ verified against the real encoder).

final ยท phrase-BPE ยท strict score โโ
attempt 1 ยท word-BPE ยท strict score โโ
10,000 vocab ยท 256 bytes + 9,744 merges
who received the merges:  English Hindi Telugu Kannada
Read it like a budget: band height = share of new merges each language was receiving at that point. English is the priciest gate (~47% of all merges to reach 1.175). The cliff is a rule, not a trend: the moment en โค 1.175 it is frozen forever โ feeding the score's minimum would widen the gap โ so every remaining merge attacks the max. The three Indic bands stay even because whoever is worst gets fed until it isn't; they leapfrog every few merges and move as one.

The score story โ and beyond 10k

Solid = inside the assignment budget. Faded = what training further would do: the final run's languages converge (peak), then the metric collapses โ the score rewards uniformity, not compression.

final ยท phrase-BPE attempt 1 ยท word-BPE

Fertility per language

Gates dashed: English < 1.2, others < 1.8. hi/te/kn descend in lockstep (the feedback loop always feeds the worst), so their lines sit exactly on top of each other โ they're drawn nested (teal widest, rose, amber core) so all three stay visible. The numbers below track the slider.

The numbers at your budget (V = โ)

Strict = akshara-aware words (เคญเคพเคฐเคค is one word โ honest). Naive \w+ = the cohort convention. โwasโ shows Attempt 1 at the same V.

Tokenize anything โ both ways

Same text, both tokenizers, at the current V. Colors mark token boundaries โ watch the final one fuse whole phrases into single tokens. Flip to token IDs to see what the model actually receives: 0โ255 are raw bytes, 256+ is the merge that created the token (its birth order). Hover a chip for the other view.

Score any Wikipedia page

Paste a Wikipedia URL โ the page is fetched with the exact same API as training, cleaned the same way, and tokenized by both tokenizers at the current V. On the four training pages this reproduces the table above (small day-to-day edit drift is expected โ the gates carry margin for it); on any other page it shows honest generalization.

ready

What we learned ยท what's next

The thinking that mattered, in four cards.

Was 10k too big or too small?

Both. Too small for word-BPE โ the four languages' merge bills total 11,020, so the wall at indic โ 2.06 is arithmetic, not tuning. Yet the corpus runs out at 16,764 word-scope merges (every word already one token). Real models solve this with 100kโ256k vocabs trained on terabytes, not one page.

The metric shapes the model

Score = 1000/(maxโmin) rewards uniformity. So the right moves are backwards-feeling: freeze the best language (improving the minimum widens the gap) and feed the worst. Past the peak, more learning makes the score worse. Always ask what a metric actually pays for.

Measure, then change the rules

We priced every language per target (merges needed) before believing any plan. 40/20/20/20 splits and multi-run token harvesting just re-slice a too-small pie โ the breakthrough was growing the pie: let merges cross spaces, a legal tokenizer-design choice with a SentencePiece precedent.

What we'd build next

A unigram-LM tokenizer (pick tokens by likelihood, drop the greedy loop) ยท vocab pruning (train big, delete useless tokens) ยท fertility on unseen pages โ phrase tokens memorize this page by design (the sanctioned cheat), so the paste-a-page box above is the honesty check.
